title: Boundary Firewalls and Internet Gateways
description: All devices within scope must be protected by a firewall at the network boundary. The firewall must be configured to block traffic that is not explicitly permitted, preventing unauthorised access to internal systems from the internet.
guidance: Cyber Essentials requires that every device in scope sits behind a firewall or equivalent boundary device. This means all internet-facing entry points must have a stateful firewall in place that inspects and controls traffic flows. The firewall must be configured and managed by the organisation, not left in a default state. Evidence of the boundary firewall's presence, configuration, and management is required for certification.
implementationGuide: Deploy a managed firewall appliance such as Cisco Meraki MX or a Palo Alto Networks PA-Series at every internet-facing network boundary. Configure the device as a stateful firewall with all inbound traffic denied by default, and only explicitly whitelisted ports and protocols permitted. The 'done' state is verified when a port scan from an external IP returns no open ports beyond those in the approved services register. A common failure mode is leaving the ISP-supplied router's built-in firewall as the sole boundary control without disabling its default permissive rules. Document the firewall's make, model, firmware version, and management access controls. Review this configuration at least quarterly and after any infrastructure change. Ensure management interfaces are not exposed to the internet under any circumstances.
exampleEvidence: Network diagram showing firewall placement at all internet-facing boundaries
Firewall configuration export showing default-deny inbound policy
External port scan report (e.g., Nmap or Qualys) confirming no unexpected open ports
Asset register entry for firewall device including make, model, and firmware version
Change management log showing last reviewed or updated firewall rules
Screenshot of firewall management console showing active rule set

title: Default Deny Inbound Rules
description: All inbound network connections must be blocked by default unless they have been explicitly permitted. The firewall policy must implement an implicit deny-all rule at the end of the inbound rule set.
guidance: A default-deny posture means the firewall drops any inbound packet that does not match an explicitly permitted rule. This is a fundamental security principle that limits the attack surface exposed to the internet. Organisations must ensure the deny-all rule is the final rule in the inbound policy and that it is not overridden by overly broad permit rules earlier in the chain.
implementationGuide: On Fortinet FortiGate or pfSense appliances, configure the inbound policy so the last rule is an explicit 'deny all' with logging enabled to capture blocked traffic. Validate the posture by running an authenticated scan with Tenable Nessus from an external vantage point and confirming zero unexpected services are reachable. The 'done' state is a clean external scan report with only approved ports visible and evidence of the deny-all rule in the exported configuration. A common failure mode is adding temporary 'any/any' permit rules during troubleshooting and forgetting to remove them. Periodically export and review the full rule base to detect rule bloat or orphaned permissive entries. Alert on any rule additions that widen the inbound attack surface.
exampleEvidence: Firewall policy export showing deny-all as the final inbound rule
External vulnerability scan report confirming no unintended open ports
Firewall log sample showing blocked inbound connection attempts
Change ticket approving any inbound permit rules added in the last quarter
Screenshot of rule ordering in firewall management GUI
Network penetration test extract confirming default-deny posture

title: Permitted Services Documentation
description: All inbound services permitted through the firewall must be documented and formally approved by an authorised person. The register must include the business justification, port/protocol, source, and destination for each permitted service.
guidance: Cyber Essentials requires that every hole in the boundary firewall has a documented business reason and a named approver. This prevents firewall rules accumulating over time without governance. The permitted services register must be kept up to date and reconciled against the live firewall rule base at regular intervals.
implementationGuide: Maintain a Permitted Services Register in a GRC platform or a version-controlled spreadsheet, cross-referencing each firewall rule on Cisco Meraki or Palo Alto Panorama with a business owner, justification, and review date. The 'done' state is when every active inbound rule maps to a register entry with no orphans in either direction. A common failure mode is rules being added via out-of-band CLI changes that never appear in the register, creating a shadow rule set. Automate reconciliation by scripting a nightly comparison between the firewall API rule export and the register. Treat any unmatched rule as a critical finding requiring immediate escalation. Review the full register at least every 12 months or after any significant infrastructure change.
exampleEvidence: Permitted Services Register listing all approved inbound rules with owner and justification
Firewall rule base export used for reconciliation against the register
Reconciliation report showing zero unmatched rules
Approval emails or change tickets authorising each permitted service
Minutes or agenda item from last quarterly firewall review meeting
Policy document defining the process for adding and approving permitted services

title: Personal Firewalls on Devices
description: A software firewall must be enabled on all user devices, including laptops and desktops, to protect them when they are used outside the corporate network boundary, such as at home or on public Wi-Fi.
guidance: Boundary firewalls only protect devices while they are on the corporate network. When staff work remotely, the device itself becomes the last line of defence. A host-based firewall on every endpoint ensures a consistent security posture regardless of the network the device is connected to. This control is mandatory under Cyber Essentials for all in-scope devices.
implementationGuide: Enforce Windows Defender Firewall via Group Policy or Microsoft Intune configuration profiles, ensuring the firewall is enabled for Domain, Private, and Public network profiles with the setting tamper-protected so end users cannot disable it. For macOS endpoints, use Jamf Pro to push and enforce the built-in Application Firewall and optionally deploy a third-party agent such as Sophos Endpoint for additional host-based controls. The 'done' state is confirmed when MDM compliance dashboards show 100% of enrolled devices reporting the firewall as active. A common failure mode is legacy devices not enrolled in MDM that run with the firewall disabled or with overly permissive inbound rules created by application installers. Audit unenrolled devices monthly and enforce enrolment as a condition of network access.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune or Jamf compliance policy screenshot enforcing firewall-on setting
MDM compliance dashboard report showing firewall status across all enrolled devices
Group Policy Object (GPO) export for Windows Defender Firewall settings
Sample endpoint configuration report from Sophos Central or equivalent
List of devices failing firewall compliance check and remediation actions taken
IT policy document requiring software firewall on all end-user devices

title: Firewall Rule Review and Approval
description: All firewall rules must be reviewed periodically to confirm they are still required and correctly configured. Reviews must be carried out by a qualified person and the outcome documented.
guidance: Firewall rule sets degrade over time as business needs change but rules are rarely removed. Periodic reviews catch stale, overly permissive, or orphaned rules before they can be exploited. Cyber Essentials expects organisations to have a defined review cycle and evidence of completed reviews.
implementationGuide: Schedule a formal firewall rule review at least every 12 months, or after any significant network change, using the management console of your Fortinet FortiManager or Cisco Firepower Management Center to export a full rule report. During the review, check each rule against the Permitted Services Register, flag rules with no associated business owner, and raise change tickets to remove or tighten any identified. The 'done' state is a signed-off review report with an audit trail of rules removed or modified as a result. A common failure mode is performing the review but making no changes, which often indicates the review was superficial rather than substantive. Assign a named rule owner to every permit rule and require annual re-certification by that owner as a condition of retention.
exampleEvidence: Completed firewall rule review report with reviewer name and date
Change tickets raised as a result of the review showing rules removed or tightened
Firewall rule base export before and after the review for comparison
Approval sign-off from CISO or IT manager on completed review
Calendar invite or scheduled task evidencing next review date
Policy document defining the firewall review cycle and scope

title: Unapproved Services Blocked
description: Any service or application that is not formally approved must be blocked at the firewall. Organisations must not permit services simply because they are in use; explicit approval and documentation are required.
guidance: Shadow IT and unauthorised services present significant risk because they are not subject to security controls or patch management. The firewall should be used as an enforcement point to block outbound access to unapproved services and inbound access from unapproved sources. Cyber Essentials requires that only necessary and approved services are permitted.
implementationGuide: Use Palo Alto Networks App-ID or Fortinet Application Control to identify and block unapproved application traffic at the firewall, complementing traditional port-based rules. Establish an approved services list and configure the firewall to block any traffic not matching a listed application or port/protocol combination. The 'done' state is a weekly application usage report showing zero blocked-but-attempted unapproved services that have not been reviewed and either approved or investigated. A common failure mode is relying solely on port blocking while unapproved applications tunnel over HTTPS on port 443, bypassing controls. Deploy SSL inspection where legally and technically feasible to prevent this evasion technique. Review application usage reports monthly and escalate anomalies to the security team.
exampleEvidence: Approved services list signed off by IT management
Firewall application control policy blocking unapproved applications
Weekly application usage or blocked traffic report
Incident record for any unapproved service detected and blocked
SSL inspection policy configuration (where deployed)
Evidence of unapproved service removal or formal approval when identified

title: Split Tunnelling Controls
description: Where VPN is used for remote access, split tunnelling must either be disabled or, if enabled for performance reasons, subject to documented risk acceptance and compensating controls to ensure internet-bound traffic from managed devices is inspected.
guidance: Split tunnelling allows VPN clients to send corporate traffic through the encrypted tunnel while routing internet traffic directly from the device. This bypasses the organisation's boundary firewall and web filtering controls, potentially exposing the device and the corporate network to threats originating from untrusted networks. Cyber Essentials requires that boundary controls are effective regardless of device location.
implementationGuide: Configure your VPN solution — such as Cisco AnyConnect or Palo Alto GlobalProtect — to enforce full tunnel mode so all traffic, including internet-bound, passes through the corporate firewall and web proxy. Where split tunnelling is operationally necessary, document the decision as a formal risk acceptance signed by the CISO, and deploy a local DNS security agent such as Cisco Umbrella Roaming Client on all affected devices to inspect and filter direct internet traffic. The 'done' state is confirmed by capturing a packet trace on a remote device and verifying all traffic egresses through the VPN tunnel. A common failure mode is VPN clients allowing users to disconnect the tunnel without enforcement, effectively disabling the control. Enforce always-on VPN with pre-logon authentication and configure the VPN client to prevent user-initiated disconnection on unmanaged networks.
exampleEvidence: VPN client configuration profile showing full tunnel or split tunnel with compensating controls
Packet capture from a remote device confirming traffic routing through corporate gateway
Cisco Umbrella or equivalent DNS security deployment report for remote devices
Signed risk acceptance document if split tunnelling is permitted
VPN usage report showing connected devices and tunnel mode in use
Policy document defining remote access and split tunnelling requirements

title: Remove or Disable Unnecessary Software and Services
description: All unnecessary software, services, and network protocols must be removed or disabled from devices to reduce the attack surface. Only software and services required for the device's business purpose should be present and active.
guidance: Every installed application and running service is a potential vulnerability. Reducing the software footprint to only what is operationally required directly reduces the number of attack vectors available to a threat actor. Cyber Essentials requires organisations to identify and remove bloatware, demo software, development tools, and any other applications not needed in a production environment.
implementationGuide: Use Microsoft Intune or Jamf Pro to push a mandatory application baseline to all managed endpoints, and configure compliance policies that flag devices with unauthorised software installed. Conduct a quarterly software audit using an agent-based tool such as Qualys Cloud Agent to generate a full software inventory and identify applications not on the approved list. The 'done' state is a clean compliance report showing all devices match the approved software baseline with no unapproved applications present. A common failure mode is standard users having local administrator rights that allow them to install software without IT oversight, bypassing baseline controls entirely. Enforce software restriction via AppLocker or equivalent, and remove local admin rights from all standard users as part of this control. Document the approved software baseline and require formal approval for any additions.
exampleEvidence: Approved software baseline list signed off by IT management
Qualys or Intune software inventory report showing installed applications per device
Compliance report identifying devices with unapproved software installed
Change tickets for software removal actions taken following audit
AppLocker or software restriction policy configuration export
MDM enrolment confirmation showing baseline applied to all in-scope devices

title: Change Default Passwords
description: All default passwords on devices, applications, and services must be changed before deployment. No device or application within scope should be reachable using a manufacturer or vendor default credential.
guidance: Default credentials are publicly documented and among the first things an attacker will try when targeting a device. Cyber Essentials is explicit that leaving default passwords in place is unacceptable on any in-scope device. This applies to routers, switches, firewalls, printers, IoT devices, applications, and operating system default accounts.
implementationGuide: During device provisioning, enforce password changes through build scripts or MDM configuration profiles in Microsoft Intune or Jamf Pro before any device is connected to the network. For network infrastructure, use CIS Benchmark hardening guides as the configuration baseline and verify compliance with a credentialed scan from Tenable Nessus. The 'done' state is a credentialed scan report showing zero instances of default credentials on any in-scope device or application. A common failure mode is printers, IP cameras, and IoT devices being deployed by facilities or other teams outside IT governance, retaining factory-default admin credentials indefinitely. Maintain an IoT and peripheral device register and include these devices in every credentialed vulnerability scan cycle. Require the procurement process to mandate credential change before production deployment.
exampleEvidence: Device build checklist requiring default password change as a deployment prerequisite
Tenable Nessus credentialed scan report showing no default credentials detected
MDM configuration profile enforcing initial password change on enrolment
IoT and peripheral device register with confirmed password-change status
Password policy document explicitly prohibiting use of default credentials
Sample change ticket for a device where default credentials were found and remediated

title: Auto-Run Disabled for Removable Media
description: Auto-run and auto-play features must be disabled for all removable media (USB drives, optical discs, etc.) on all in-scope devices. This prevents malicious code from executing automatically when media is inserted.
guidance: Auto-run has been a primary malware delivery mechanism for decades. Disabling it removes the ability for an attacker to execute code simply by having a user insert a prepared USB drive. Cyber Essentials requires this control on all in-scope Windows and macOS devices. Where removable media use is not required for a business function, blocking it entirely is the stronger control.
implementationGuide: Configure Group Policy (Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > AutoPlay Policies) to disable AutoRun on all drive types, and deploy this via Active Directory or Microsoft Intune to all Windows endpoints. For macOS, use Jamf Pro to push a configuration profile disabling the auto-open behaviour for external volumes. The 'done' state is confirmed by inserting a test USB drive on a managed device and verifying no auto-run prompt or automatic execution occurs, with MDM compliance reporting the setting as enforced. A common failure mode is the policy applying to domain-joined machines but not to devices that have been removed from the domain or provisioned outside the standard build process. Ensure MDM compliance policies flag and remediate non-compliant devices automatically. Consider deploying USB port control software such as Sophos Device Control to also block unapproved removable media entirely.
exampleEvidence: Group Policy Object export showing AutoPlay and AutoRun disabled for all drive types
Microsoft Intune or Jamf configuration profile enforcing the auto-run setting
MDM compliance report confirming auto-run disabled across all enrolled devices
Test result log showing no auto-execution on USB insertion on a managed device
Sophos Device Control or equivalent policy preventing use of unapproved USB devices
Policy document requiring auto-run to be disabled on all corporate endpoints

title: Password Policy — Minimum Length and Complexity
description: A password policy must be defined and technically enforced that requires minimum length of at least 8 characters (or 12 characters for administrator accounts) and either complexity requirements or length requirements per NCSC guidance.
guidance: Weak passwords remain one of the most exploited vulnerabilities in enterprise environments. Cyber Essentials requires a technically enforced password policy — not just a written policy that relies on user compliance. The NCSC's current guidance favours longer passwords over complex but short ones, and recommends against frequent mandatory rotations that drive users towards weak predictable passwords.
implementationGuide: Enforce password policy settings via Active Directory Fine-Grained Password Policies or Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) Conditional Access and Authentication Strength policies, setting a minimum of 12 characters for all accounts and 15 for privileged accounts. Complement this with Microsoft Intune device compliance policies that require a compliant PIN or password on all managed mobile and desktop devices. The 'done' state is a Group Policy or Entra ID policy report confirming the minimum length setting is active and no accounts with non-compliant passwords exist in the directory. A common failure mode is a strong policy defined in Active Directory but local accounts on workstations or servers with separate, weaker password policies that go unmanaged. Audit local account password policies on all devices using a script or Qualys credentialed scan and enforce domain policy application.
exampleEvidence: Active Directory Fine-Grained Password Policy or Entra ID Authentication Strength configuration
Microsoft Intune compliance policy screenshot enforcing minimum password length on devices
Audit report confirming no accounts with passwords below the minimum length
Group Policy Object export showing domain password policy settings
Password policy document aligned to NCSC guidance
Evidence of local account password policy audit on servers and workstations

title: Screen Lock After Inactivity
description: All devices must be configured to lock automatically after a defined period of inactivity, requiring re-authentication to regain access. This prevents unauthorised access to an unattended device.
guidance: Unattended unlocked devices are a physical and logical security risk. A screen lock policy ensures that even if a device is left unlocked briefly, access is revoked within a defined timeout. Cyber Essentials requires this control to be technically enforced, not merely user-configured. Typical acceptable timeouts are 10 minutes or less for high-risk environments.
implementationGuide: Deploy a screen lock inactivity policy via Microsoft Intune (Windows and mobile devices) and Jamf Pro (macOS and iOS) setting the maximum inactivity timeout to no more than 10 minutes before the screen locks and requires authentication. For Windows, also enforce via Group Policy (Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies) as a belt-and-braces control. The 'done' state is an MDM compliance dashboard showing 100% of enrolled devices reporting screen lock as configured and compliant. A common failure mode is presentation or kiosk PCs being exempted from the policy for convenience and then being left accessible indefinitely; all exemptions must be formally risk-accepted and documented. Review exemption lists quarterly and ensure kiosk devices are subject to physical access controls if the screen lock policy is waived.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune device compliance policy showing screen lock timeout enforcement
Jamf Pro configuration profile screenshot for macOS and iOS screen lock settings
Group Policy Object export for screen saver timeout and password-on-resume settings
MDM compliance report showing screen lock compliance rate across enrolled devices
Register of devices exempted from screen lock policy with signed risk acceptance
IT security policy document specifying maximum inactivity timeout requirements

title: Software Installation Restricted to Authorised Users
description: Only authorised users, typically IT administrators, must be permitted to install software on in-scope devices. Standard users must not have the rights to install unapproved software.
guidance: Allowing standard users to install software is one of the most common causes of malware infection and security policy violations. Restricting software installation rights to IT administrators ensures that only approved, verified software runs on corporate devices. Cyber Essentials requires this to be technically enforced, not reliant on user awareness training alone.
implementationGuide: Remove local administrator privileges from all standard user accounts using Microsoft Intune's Account Protection policies or Jamf Pro's Privileges app, and enforce software installation restrictions via AppLocker or Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policies. For macOS, configure the device to require administrator authentication for any new application installation. The 'done' state is confirmed when a test standard user account cannot install a signed application without IT credentials, and the MDM compliance policy reports non-admin status on all standard user devices. A common failure mode is users discovering a way to escalate privileges through a vulnerable local service or by logging in as a local admin account that was not removed during the build process. Run regular privilege escalation audits using CrowdStrike Falcon or equivalent EDR tooling to detect and alert on unexpected privilege changes.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune Account Protection policy removing local admin rights from standard users
AppLocker or WDAC policy export restricting software installation to admins
Test result showing standard user unable to install software without admin credentials
Jamf Pro configuration profile for macOS admin authentication requirement
CrowdStrike Falcon or EDR alert report for privilege escalation events
IT policy document defining who is authorised to install software and the approval process

title: Secure Configuration Baseline Documented and Applied
description: A secure configuration baseline must be defined, documented, and applied to all in-scope devices. The baseline must be derived from recognised guidance such as CIS Benchmarks or vendor hardening guides.
guidance: Operating systems and applications are shipped with default configurations optimised for compatibility and ease of use, not security. A hardened configuration baseline closes known insecure defaults, disables unnecessary features, and applies security settings appropriate for the organisation's risk profile. Cyber Essentials requires evidence that a baseline has been intentionally defined and applied.
implementationGuide: Define device configuration baselines aligned to CIS Benchmark Level 1 for each platform in scope (Windows 11, macOS Sonoma, iOS/Android), and deploy them as configuration profiles via Microsoft Intune for Windows and iOS/Android, and Jamf Pro for macOS. Use Qualys Policy Compliance or Tenable.sc to continuously assess all devices against the baseline and report on drift. The 'done' state is a compliance dashboard showing baseline adherence rates above 95% across all device classes, with any failures tracked as remediation items. A common failure mode is defining the baseline in documentation but not encoding it in MDM policy, relying on manual build checklists that degrade over time. Automate baseline enforcement through MDM and treat any manual override as a change requiring formal approval.
exampleEvidence: CIS Benchmark or hardening baseline document for each device platform in scope
Microsoft Intune or Jamf Pro configuration profile library implementing the baseline
Qualys Policy Compliance or Tenable scan report showing baseline adherence scores
Remediation tracker for devices failing baseline compliance checks
Change management process requiring baseline impact assessment for configuration changes
MDM enrolment report confirming baseline profiles applied to all enrolled devices

title: Guest and Anonymous Accounts Disabled
description: All guest, anonymous, and shared accounts must be disabled on in-scope devices and systems. Every user must authenticate with a uniquely assigned account that can be attributed to an individual.
guidance: Guest and anonymous accounts bypass normal authentication controls and make activity attribution impossible. Shared accounts present similar risks because actions cannot be tied to a specific individual, undermining accountability. Cyber Essentials requires all such accounts to be disabled, not merely secured with a password, unless there is a specific documented business case for a shared account with compensating controls.
implementationGuide: Audit all Active Directory and local accounts on Windows devices using PowerShell scripts or Qualys credentialed scans to identify any guest, anonymous, or shared accounts, and disable or delete them. Enforce the disabling of the built-in Windows Guest account via Group Policy (Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options) and verify via Microsoft Intune compliance policies. The 'done' state is an account audit report showing zero active guest or anonymous accounts across all in-scope systems. A common failure mode is service accounts created with generic names (e.g., 'service', 'admin', 'backup') that are treated as shared accounts and lack individual ownership. Require all service accounts to have a named owner in Active Directory and schedule quarterly reviews. On macOS, use Jamf Pro to enforce the disabling of the Guest user account in the configuration profile.
exampleEvidence: PowerShell or Qualys account audit report showing no active guest or anonymous accounts
Group Policy Object export disabling the built-in Windows Guest account
Microsoft Intune compliance policy check for guest account status
Jamf Pro configuration profile disabling the macOS Guest user
Active Directory account review report with service account ownership confirmed
IT policy document prohibiting guest and shared accounts except with formal approval

title: Unique User Accounts — No Account Sharing
description: Every user must be assigned a unique individual account. Sharing of accounts between multiple users is strictly prohibited on all in-scope systems and devices.
guidance: Account sharing makes it impossible to attribute actions to specific individuals, undermines security monitoring and incident investigation, and inflates access rights because the account must accommodate all users' combined requirements. Cyber Essentials requires every person to have their own named account on all systems they use.
implementationGuide: Enforce unique account provisioning through your Identity Governance tool or Active Directory provisioning workflow, ensuring every joiner receives a uniquely named account tied to their employee record before their first day. Audit Active Directory and local system accounts monthly using PowerShell or Qualys credentialed scans, looking for accounts with names suggesting shared use (e.g., 'reception', 'shift1', 'accounts'). The 'done' state is a clean account audit report with zero shared accounts and every account traceable to a named individual in the HR system. A common failure mode is operational teams creating shared accounts for shift-based roles because shared passwords are easier to manage — address this by deploying CyberArk or a password vault that provides individual check-out of shared credentials with full audit logging. Document any approved exceptions with a risk acceptance signed by the data owner.
exampleEvidence: Active Directory account audit report confirming all accounts map to named individuals
HR system extract cross-referenced against AD accounts showing no orphaned shared accounts
Account provisioning process document requiring unique account per user
Sample onboarding ticket showing unique account created for a new joiner
CyberArk or password vault report showing shared credential check-out audit trail
IT policy document prohibiting account sharing

title: Principle of Least Privilege — Standard User Accounts
description: Users must be given only the access rights necessary to perform their job functions. Day-to-day work must be carried out using standard user accounts with no administrative privileges.
guidance: The principle of least privilege is a foundational security control that limits the damage an attacker or compromised account can cause. A standard user account that cannot install software or modify system settings significantly reduces the blast radius of a phishing attack or malware infection. Cyber Essentials requires all users to work with the minimum access necessary for their role.
implementationGuide: Conduct an access rights review across all business systems using Microsoft Entra ID access reviews or a third-party IGA tool, removing any access that cannot be justified by the user's current role. Configure Microsoft Intune Account Protection policies to ensure standard user accounts have no local admin rights on Windows devices. The 'done' state is an access review report showing all accounts have access aligned to their job role with no excess permissions, and the MDM compliance dashboard confirming zero standard users have local admin rights. A common failure mode is access rights accumulating over time as users change roles without old access being removed — implement automated role-based access revocation through your ITSM and HR system integration. Schedule and evidence role-based access reviews at least annually.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Entra ID access review report confirming role-aligned access assignments
Microsoft Intune compliance report showing zero standard users with local admin rights
Access rights review schedule and completed review evidence
HR-triggered access revocation tickets for users who changed roles
Role-based access control matrix defining entitlements per job function
IT policy document describing least-privilege access requirements

title: Administrative Access Controlled — Dedicated Admin Accounts
description: Administrative accounts must be used only for administrative tasks. Staff who require admin access must use a separate dedicated admin account for those tasks and their standard account for all day-to-day work.
guidance: Using an administrator account for everyday tasks such as email and web browsing dramatically increases the risk of malware gaining persistent, privileged access to the system. Cyber Essentials requires segregation of standard and administrative accounts so that the elevated privilege is only exercised when deliberately invoked for a specific administrative task.
implementationGuide: Provision separate privileged accounts in Active Directory for all users requiring admin access, naming them clearly (e.g., using a 'priv-' or '-adm' suffix), and enforce that these accounts cannot log on interactively to standard workstations by applying Group Policy logon restrictions. Integrate privileged accounts with CyberArk Privileged Access Manager or Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) so that admin rights are time-limited and require justification before elevation. The 'done' state is confirmed when the privileged account audit shows all admin tasks are performed through dedicated accounts with no standard accounts holding admin group membership. A common failure mode is IT staff using their privileged account to check email or browse the internet, negating the segregation benefit — enforce this through technical controls such as blocking privileged accounts from accessing email or internet via Conditional Access policies.
exampleEvidence: Active Directory report listing all privileged accounts and their associated standard accounts
CyberArk or Entra PIM configuration showing time-limited admin access with justification
Group Policy restricting privileged account interactive logon to admin jump servers
Conditional Access policy preventing privileged accounts from accessing email or the internet
Privileged account usage log showing admin tasks performed only through dedicated accounts
IT policy document defining the use of separate standard and admin accounts

title: Admin Account Audit — Regular Review of Administrative Rights
description: All accounts with administrative or elevated privileges must be reviewed regularly to confirm the access remains necessary, appropriate, and assigned to the correct individuals.
guidance: Privileged accounts are high-value targets and their proliferation over time without governance is a major risk. Regular reviews catch accounts belonging to departed staff, role changes that made the privilege unnecessary, and unauthorised privilege grants. Cyber Essentials requires periodic review evidence for all accounts with administrative rights.
implementationGuide: Configure Microsoft Entra ID Access Reviews to automatically trigger quarterly reviews of all groups with administrative roles, sending review requests to the named owners of each privileged group. For on-premises Active Directory, schedule a quarterly PowerShell audit of all members of Domain Admins, Local Admins, and other privileged groups, cross-referencing against the current HR headcount. The 'done' state is a completed review report for each privileged group with sign-off from the group owner and evidence of any access removed as a result. A common failure mode is reviews being completed by the IT team reviewing their own access — require business owners or line managers to approve the continued need for each privileged account to ensure independence. Use Tenable.io AD Security or equivalent to detect privilege escalation and unreviewed admin accounts in real time.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Entra ID Access Review report for privileged roles completed in the last quarter
PowerShell audit report listing all members of privileged Active Directory groups
HR leavers list cross-referenced against privileged account audit showing no orphaned accounts
Access revocation tickets raised as a result of the admin account review
Tenable AD Security or equivalent alert report for unreviewed privileged account changes
Signed review completion sign-off from business or group owner

title: Privileged Access Controls — Special Controls on Admin Accounts
description: Additional technical controls must be applied to privileged accounts to reduce the risk of compromise, including restrictions on where and how admin accounts can be used, and monitoring of privileged activity.
guidance: Privileged accounts require stronger controls than standard accounts because their compromise has a disproportionate impact. Beyond basic MFA, organisations should restrict where admin accounts can authenticate from, limit the systems they can access, and maintain detailed audit logs of all privileged activity. Cyber Essentials Plus assessors will verify these controls are technically implemented.
implementationGuide: Deploy Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) with just-in-time activation requiring MFA and business justification for all Azure and Microsoft 365 admin roles. For on-premises infrastructure, route all privileged sessions through a Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) or CyberArk session management so that every admin action is recorded and can be reviewed. The 'done' state is a PIM audit log showing all admin role activations include an MFA step and a recorded justification, with no direct persistent admin role assignments outside PIM. A common failure mode is permanent admin role assignments existing alongside PIM-managed just-in-time assignments, providing an uncontrolled elevated path that bypasses the JIT approval workflow. Audit for permanent role assignments monthly and convert them to eligible assignments.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Entra PIM configuration showing eligible (JIT) assignments for all admin roles
PIM audit log showing admin activations with MFA and justification recorded
CyberArk session recording sample demonstrating privileged session capture
Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) build specification and deployment list
Conditional Access policy restricting admin account sign-in to approved locations or devices
Report confirming no permanent admin role assignments outside PIM-managed workflows

title: Password Manager Use and Multi-Factor Authentication
description: Users must be provided with and encouraged to use a password manager to generate and store unique, strong passwords for each account. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) must be enabled for all user accounts where the platform supports it.
guidance: Password reuse is one of the most common causes of account compromise. A password manager eliminates the need for users to remember multiple passwords, removing the primary motivation for reuse. Combined with MFA, these controls provide substantial protection against credential-based attacks even if a password is compromised. Cyber Essentials expects MFA to be deployed on all accounts accessing internet-facing services.
implementationGuide: Deploy an enterprise password manager such as 1Password Business or Bitwarden Teams to all staff, and enforce its use via onboarding training and policy acknowledgement. Enforce MFA via Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policies requiring phishing-resistant authentication (FIDO2 or Microsoft Authenticator number matching) for all cloud service access and for any account accessing the corporate network. The 'done' state is a Conditional Access report showing 100% of enabled accounts are subject to an MFA policy with no MFA exclusions for active user accounts. A common failure mode is a small number of 'break-glass' or service accounts being permanently excluded from MFA requirements without compensating controls, creating exploitable exceptions. Document all MFA exclusions with a risk acceptance, implement IP-based conditional access restrictions on excluded accounts, and review the list quarterly.
exampleEvidence: 1Password Business or Bitwarden deployment report showing all staff licensed and enrolled
Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policy enforcing MFA for all user sign-ins
MFA registration report showing percentage of users enrolled in phishing-resistant MFA
Conditional Access named location policy restricting MFA-excluded accounts
IT policy document mandating MFA and password manager use
List of MFA exclusions with signed risk acceptance and review date

title: Multi-Factor Authentication for Remote Access
description: Multi-factor authentication must be enforced for all remote access connections to in-scope systems, including VPN, remote desktop, cloud management portals, and any internet-facing service.
guidance: Remote access represents the highest-risk authentication scenario because the organisation cannot rely on network-level controls to limit exposure. MFA adds a critical second layer of defence, ensuring that a stolen password alone cannot grant access to corporate systems from an external network. Cyber Essentials is explicit that MFA is required for all remote access.
implementationGuide: Enforce MFA for VPN authentication by integrating your Cisco AnyConnect or Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN gateway with Microsoft Entra ID as the identity provider, applying a Conditional Access policy that requires MFA for any connection from outside a trusted named location. For RDP and server management, require Azure AD-joined devices or deploy Remote Desktop Gateway with MFA enforcement via NPS extension. The 'done' state is a Conditional Access sign-in log showing 100% of remote access authentications required and completed MFA, with no successful logins recorded from external IPs without MFA. A common failure mode is legacy VPN clients or appliances that do not support modern authentication protocols, making Entra ID integration impossible and leaving MFA ungoverned. Audit and remediate legacy authentication usage via the Entra ID sign-in logs and enforce blocking of legacy authentication protocols via Conditional Access.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for remote access sign-ins
Entra ID sign-in log extract showing MFA required for all external IP authentications
VPN gateway configuration showing Entra ID as the identity provider with MFA enforced
Conditional Access policy blocking legacy authentication protocols
Remote Desktop Gateway or Azure Bastion MFA configuration screenshot
IT policy document mandating MFA for all remote access methods

title: Anti-Malware Software Installed and Active on All Devices
description: Anti-malware software must be installed, active, and up to date on all in-scope devices. The software must be configured to scan and protect the device in real time.
guidance: Anti-malware is a fundamental baseline control that provides protection against known threats. Cyber Essentials requires that every device in scope runs an active anti-malware solution. Cloud-hosted or SaaS devices may use platform-native protections provided they meet the functional requirements of detection, real-time scanning, and central management.
implementationGuide: Deploy Microsoft Defender for Endpoint or CrowdStrike Falcon to all Windows and macOS devices, managed centrally through their respective cloud management consoles. For mobile devices, ensure Defender for Endpoint or Sophos Intercept X for Mobile is deployed via Microsoft Intune. The 'done' state is a central management console report showing 100% of in-scope devices have an active, healthy anti-malware agent with no devices in a 'sensor offline' or 'no recent check-in' state. A common failure mode is devices coming back from repair or rebuild without the anti-malware agent being reinstalled, creating a protection gap that goes unnoticed if device reporting is not monitored actively. Configure automated alerts for any device not checking in within 24 hours and include agent health as a condition in the MDM compliance policy.
exampleEvidence: CrowdStrike Falcon or Defender for Endpoint dashboard showing 100% device coverage
MDM compliance policy enforcing anti-malware installation as a device health requirement
Agent health report listing all devices with last check-in timestamp
Alert configuration showing notifications for offline or unhealthy anti-malware agents
Anti-malware deployment policy document defining supported products and coverage requirements
Sample onboarding checklist confirming anti-malware installed before device handover

title: Anti-Malware Definitions Updated at Least Daily
description: Anti-malware signature definitions must be updated at least daily on all in-scope devices. Devices with outdated definitions are not considered compliant.
guidance: New malware variants are released constantly, and signature-based detection is only effective when definitions are current. A device with definitions more than 24 hours old may not detect recently released threats. Cyber Essentials requires daily definition updates as a minimum, and most modern solutions update multiple times per day by default.
implementationGuide: Configure Microsoft Defender for Endpoint or Malwarebytes Nebula to update security intelligence automatically, and set the update frequency to at least every 4 hours using the anti-malware policy settings in Microsoft Intune or the Malwarebytes management console. Monitor definition age via the management dashboard and configure automated alerts when any device's definitions are older than 24 hours. The 'done' state is a definitions currency report showing 100% of active devices have definitions updated within the last 24 hours. A common failure mode is devices that are rarely switched on (e.g., hot-desk PCs, meeting room computers) accumulating stale definitions because they are not regularly connected to the update source. Implement a policy requiring these devices to update before use and include definition age in daily health dashboards. Ensure devices on remote networks can reach cloud update endpoints without being blocked by local DNS or firewall controls.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint or Malwarebytes definition currency report
Intune or management console policy setting for automatic definition update frequency
Alert log showing notifications triggered for devices with definitions older than 24 hours
Dashboard screenshot confirming all active devices have current definitions
Network rule confirming update endpoint URLs are reachable from all device locations
Anti-malware policy document specifying minimum definition update frequency

title: Anti-Malware Real-Time Protection Enabled
description: Real-time or on-access scanning must be enabled on all in-scope devices. Real-time protection detects and blocks malware as it attempts to execute or write to disk, rather than relying solely on scheduled scans.
guidance: Scheduled scans alone are insufficient because they may run hours or days after a malicious file arrives on the device. Real-time protection intercepts threats at the point of execution, providing the fastest possible response to new infections. Cyber Essentials requires real-time protection to be active and not disabled by users.
implementationGuide: Enable and tamper-protect real-time protection settings in CrowdStrike Falcon or Microsoft Defender for Endpoint via their respective cloud policy consoles, ensuring users cannot disable the protection through the device UI or via registry changes. Configure Defender's tamper protection feature in the Defender for Endpoint portal and enforce it through an Intune security baseline policy. The 'done' state is a management console report showing real-time protection is active and tamper-protected on 100% of enrolled devices. A common failure mode is IT staff disabling real-time protection during application installations or troubleshooting and forgetting to re-enable it, leaving the device unprotected until the next policy refresh cycle. Configure automatic re-enabling of real-time protection after 1 hour if manually disabled and alert the security team immediately when tampering is detected. Log and investigate all instances of real-time protection being disabled.
exampleEvidence: Defender for Endpoint or CrowdStrike policy showing real-time protection enabled and tamper-protected
Intune security baseline policy enforcing real-time scanning settings
Management console report confirming real-time protection active on all enrolled devices
Alert log showing tamper-protection events detected and investigated
IT procedure requiring re-enabling real-time protection after any diagnostic disabling
Anti-malware policy document prohibiting end-user disabling of real-time protection

title: Potentially Unwanted Application (PUA) Scanning Enabled
description: Anti-malware solutions must be configured to detect and block Potentially Unwanted Applications (PUAs), including adware, browser hijackers, and bundled software that may not be strictly malicious but poses a security or compliance risk.
guidance: PUAs often arrive bundled with legitimate software or as click-through installers and can introduce significant risks including data exfiltration, browser modification, and performance degradation. Enabling PUA detection expands the protective coverage of anti-malware beyond traditional malware to include borderline applications that represent a risk. Cyber Essentials assessors may check that PUA protection is enabled and not just default-configured.
implementationGuide: Enable PUA protection in Microsoft Defender for Endpoint using the 'Block' mode via Intune's endpoint protection profile, and configure Malwarebytes Nebula's PUP/PUA detection to block rather than warn on detection. Set up automated remediation so detected PUAs are quarantined without user intervention and the event is reported to the central management console. The 'done' state is confirmed by deploying a known PUA test file (e.g., EICAR PUA test) and verifying it is blocked and an alert is generated in the management console. A common failure mode is PUA detection being configured in 'audit' mode rather than 'block' mode, meaning threats are logged but not remediated. Review PUA detection settings after any major product update as some updates reset custom settings to defaults. Document PUA policy settings and include them in the quarterly security configuration review.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune endpoint protection profile showing PUA protection set to Block mode
Malwarebytes Nebula PUP/PUA policy screenshot confirming block on detection
PUA test result log showing detection and quarantine of a test PUA file
Anti-malware management console report of PUA detections and automated remediation actions
Policy review record confirming PUA settings checked after product update
Anti-malware policy document requiring PUA blocking enabled on all devices

title: Malware Sandboxing and Behavioural Detection
description: Anti-malware solutions should incorporate behavioural detection or sandboxing capabilities to detect and block threats that are not detected by signature-based scanning alone, including zero-day exploits and fileless malware.
guidance: Signature-based detection is increasingly insufficient as threat actors use polymorphic malware and living-off-the-land techniques to evade traditional AV. Behavioural detection and sandboxing analyse what code does rather than what it looks like, providing protection against previously unknown threats. While not a base Cyber Essentials requirement, it is required for Cyber Essentials Plus and represents current best practice.
implementationGuide: Enable and configure CrowdStrike Falcon's AI-powered behavioural prevention policies to block suspicious process behaviours including credential dumping, lateral movement, and script-based attacks, without requiring a signature match. Alternatively, deploy Microsoft Defender for Endpoint's advanced threat protection with EDR block mode enabled. The 'done' state is a threat protection report showing behavioural detections being made and blocked on endpoints, demonstrated by running a benign red-team simulation tool and confirming the detection. A common failure mode is deploying the EDR solution in 'detect only' mode to avoid disrupting business applications, leaving the behavioural engine unable to prevent threats it detects. Invest time in tuning the policy to reduce false positives before enabling block mode, and run the solution in audit mode for no more than 30 days before switching to prevention.
exampleEvidence: CrowdStrike Falcon behavioural prevention policy configuration in block mode
Defender for Endpoint EDR block mode configuration and threat protection report
Simulation or red team test result demonstrating behavioural detection in action
Alert log showing behavioural detections blocked without prior signature match
False positive review log demonstrating tuning of behavioural engine policies
Security policy document requiring behavioural detection to be in block mode

title: Application Allowlisting
description: For Cyber Essentials Plus, an application allowlisting control must be in place that permits only approved, explicitly listed applications to execute on in-scope devices. All other application execution must be blocked.
guidance: Application allowlisting is a powerful malware prevention control that prevents any unapproved code from executing, regardless of whether it matches a malware signature. It is particularly effective against zero-day threats and script-based attacks. This control is required for Cyber Essentials Plus certification and is considered advanced practice for standard Cyber Essentials.
implementationGuide: Implement Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) via Microsoft Intune using a policy in enforced mode, built from the device's existing software baseline and publisher certificate rules, blocking any unsigned or unapproved executables. Alternatively, deploy AppLocker via Group Policy for environments that cannot support WDAC. The 'done' state is confirmed when a test unsigned executable cannot run on a managed device and the block event is visible in the WDAC or AppLocker event log. A common failure mode is application allowlisting being applied too broadly without sufficient testing, causing legitimate business applications to be blocked and significant operational disruption. Pilot the policy in audit mode on a representative device set for at least two weeks before enforcing, resolve all false positives, and deploy in enforcement mode progressively. Maintain a break-glass process for emergency whitelisting requests.
exampleEvidence: WDAC or AppLocker policy XML showing enforced mode configuration
Microsoft Intune policy deployment report for WDAC configuration
Test result showing unsigned executable blocked on a managed device
WDAC or AppLocker event log extract showing blocked execution events
Pilot audit mode report used to identify and resolve false positives before enforcement
Emergency whitelisting request process document with approval workflow

title: Operating Systems Kept Up to Date — 14-Day Patching
description: Security patches and updates for all operating systems must be applied within 14 days of release. This applies to all in-scope devices including servers, desktops, laptops, and mobile devices.
guidance: Unpatched operating systems are the most common attack vector exploited by ransomware groups and nation-state actors. Cyber Essentials requires a 14-day maximum window for applying high and critical OS patches. The organisation must have a tested, automated patching process and evidence that patches are being applied within the required timeframe.
implementationGuide: Configure Windows Update for Business policies via Microsoft Intune to apply security patches within 7 days of release, with a 2-day test ring ensuring patches are validated before broad deployment. Monitor patch compliance using WSUS reporting or the Intune Update Compliance workbook in Azure Monitor, tracking the percentage of devices patched within 14 days. The 'done' state is a patch compliance report showing 100% of devices have the current OS security patches installed within the 14-day window. A common failure mode is devices that are frequently offline (e.g., laptops used by field staff) repeatedly missing the 14-day window because they are not connected long enough to complete updates. Enforce a policy requiring devices to connect to the corporate network or cloud update service at least every 7 days and flag non-compliant devices for remediation.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune Update Compliance report showing patch installation dates vs. release dates
Windows Update for Business policy showing deployment ring configuration and deadlines
WSUS or Intune patch compliance dashboard showing percentage of devices patched within 14 days
List of devices outside the 14-day patching window with remediation tickets
Patch management policy document defining the 14-day requirement and exception process
Azure Monitor alert configuration for devices exceeding the patch compliance deadline

title: Applications Kept Up to Date — Third-Party Software Patching
description: Security updates for all installed applications, including third-party software, must be applied within 14 days of release. This applies to all applications on in-scope devices, not just the operating system.
guidance: Third-party applications such as browsers, PDF readers, and productivity software are frequently exploited through known vulnerabilities. Many organisations focus on OS patching but neglect application updates, leaving significant attack surface unaddressed. Cyber Essentials requires the same 14-day patching standard to be applied to all applications, not just the operating system.
implementationGuide: Deploy Qualys Patch Management or Ivanti Patch for Endpoints to automate third-party application patching, covering the most commonly exploited applications including Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Adobe Acrobat, and Microsoft Office. Configure automated patch deployment with a 7-day test ring to validate application compatibility before broad rollout. The 'done' state is a third-party patch compliance report from Qualys or Ivanti showing all in-scope applications are within one patch version of the current release, with no critical CVEs unpatched beyond 14 days. A common failure mode is treating browser patching as covered by auto-update when Chrome or Firefox auto-update is blocked by corporate proxy settings, silently leaving browsers unpatched for extended periods. Verify browser auto-update channels are accessible from all device locations and supplement with centrally managed patch deployment.
exampleEvidence: Qualys Patch Management or Ivanti patch compliance report for third-party applications
List of in-scope applications with current version vs. latest available version comparison
Automated patch deployment policy configuration for third-party applications
CVE tracking report showing no critical application vulnerabilities unpatched beyond 14 days
Browser version compliance report showing all Chrome and Firefox instances are current
Patch management policy document including third-party application patching requirements

title: Unsupported Software Identified and Removed
description: Software that is no longer supported by its vendor and therefore no longer receives security updates must be identified and either removed from in-scope systems or isolated with compensating controls and a documented risk acceptance.
guidance: End-of-life software is permanently vulnerable because security patches will never be issued for newly discovered flaws. Cyber Essentials assessors look specifically for unsupported operating systems and applications. If such software cannot be removed immediately, a credible and time-bound migration plan must be in place with interim compensating controls documented.
implementationGuide: Run a Tenable Nessus or Qualys vulnerability scan across all in-scope devices and filter results for detections indicating end-of-life or unsupported software, including Windows 7, Windows Server 2012, and applications beyond their vendor support date. Cross-reference findings against the software inventory to identify all instances and assign remediation owners. The 'done' state is a scan report showing zero instances of unsupported software on any in-scope device, or documented risk acceptances with migration plans for any exceptions. A common failure mode is unsupported software persisting because it is embedded in a business-critical process with no upgrade path — address this by escalating to the board as a risk register item requiring funded remediation, and apply network isolation and enhanced monitoring as interim controls. Review the unsupported software register quarterly.
exampleEvidence: Tenable Nessus or Qualys scan report filtered for end-of-life software detections
Software inventory extract highlighting applications beyond vendor support dates
Remediation tickets for all unsupported software instances identified
Risk register entries for any unsupported software that cannot be immediately removed
Migration plan with timeline for replacing any remaining unsupported software
Network isolation documentation for systems running unsupported software pending migration

title: Automatic Updates Enabled Where Possible
description: Automatic update mechanisms must be enabled for all operating systems and applications where the feature is available, to minimise the window of exposure to known vulnerabilities.
guidance: Enabling automatic updates removes the dependency on manual processes that are prone to delay and human error. Where automatic updates cannot be used due to compatibility testing requirements, a compensating manual process with defined timelines must be in place. Cyber Essentials strongly favours automatic updating as the default posture.
implementationGuide: Configure Windows Update for Business via Microsoft Intune to automatically apply security updates with no option for end users to indefinitely defer, setting a maximum deferral period of 7 days for quality updates. For macOS, use Jamf Pro to enforce automatic security response updates and critical security patches. The 'done' state is an Intune and Jamf compliance report showing automatic updates are enabled and users cannot defer beyond the defined maximum period on any enrolled device. A common failure mode is IT teams setting broad exclusions for server workloads to avoid unplanned reboots, resulting in servers going months without applying security patches. Implement a defined server maintenance window schedule and automate patching during those windows using WSUS or a patch orchestration tool, removing the need for blanket update exclusions.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune Windows Update for Business policy showing automatic update configuration
Jamf Pro policy screenshot enforcing automatic macOS security updates
MDM compliance report confirming automatic updates cannot be disabled by end users
Server maintenance window schedule with automated patching configuration
WSUS or patch management tool configuration showing server patching schedule
Patch management policy document requiring automatic updates by default

title: Software Inventory Maintained
description: An up-to-date inventory of all software installed on in-scope devices must be maintained. The inventory must include the software name, version, vendor, and the device it is installed on.
guidance: An accurate software inventory is a prerequisite for effective patch management. Without knowing what software is installed and at what version, it is impossible to systematically identify missing patches or unsupported software. Cyber Essentials assessors will look for evidence of a maintained inventory that is used to drive the patching programme.
implementationGuide: Deploy Qualys Cloud Agent or use Microsoft Intune's discovered apps inventory to automatically collect software installation data from all enrolled devices, generating a central software inventory that is updated in real time. Configure automated alerting when new software is detected outside the approved baseline. The 'done' state is an inventory report showing complete software coverage across all in-scope devices, with version information for every installed application and a timestamp confirming the inventory was updated within the last 24 hours. A common failure mode is the inventory tool not covering all device types — particularly servers, Linux devices, and BYOD — creating blind spots where unpatched software goes undetected. Ensure agent deployment extends to all device types in scope and use network-based scanning as a complementary control to identify devices without agents installed.
exampleEvidence: Qualys Cloud Agent or Intune discovered apps inventory report with version and device details
Inventory completeness report cross-referencing enrolled devices against asset register
Automated alert configuration for newly detected software outside the approved baseline
Software inventory showing last-updated timestamp confirming real-time data collection
Network scan report identifying devices without inventory agents installed
Patch management policy document requiring a maintained and current software inventory

title: Patch Prioritisation Process — Critical Patches Applied Urgently
description: A defined process must be in place for identifying and prioritising critical security patches, with critical and high-severity patches applied more urgently than the standard 14-day window where active exploitation is known.
guidance: Not all patches carry equal risk. When CISA or NCSC issue alerts for actively exploited vulnerabilities, the 14-day window may be too long. Organisations should monitor threat intelligence sources and be prepared to emergency-patch within 24-72 hours when a vulnerability is being actively exploited in the wild. Cyber Essentials expects a proportionate response to patch criticality.
implementationGuide: Subscribe to NCSC Early Warning Service and configure Qualys VMDR or Tenable.io to prioritise vulnerabilities using threat intelligence feeds (e.g., CISA KEV catalogue integration) that flag actively exploited CVEs for immediate remediation. Define a tiered patching SLA: P1 (actively exploited/CISA KEV) within 48 hours, P2 (CVSS 9.0+) within 7 days, P3 (standard) within 14 days. The 'done' state is a vulnerability ageing report showing all P1 and P2 vulnerabilities are remediated within their SLA with zero exceptions older than the defined window. A common failure mode is having a defined SLA on paper but no automated enforcement mechanism, causing critical vulnerabilities to slip through because no one actively monitors the SLA compliance dashboard. Configure automated escalation to the CISO when a P1 vulnerability exceeds 24 hours without a confirmed remediation plan.
exampleEvidence: Patch prioritisation policy document defining SLA tiers based on exploitability and severity
NCSC Early Warning Service subscription confirmation
Qualys VMDR or Tenable CISA KEV integration showing actively exploited CVEs flagged
Vulnerability ageing report confirming P1/P2 vulnerabilities remediated within SLA
Escalation alert log for vulnerabilities exceeding the defined emergency patching SLA
Incident record for an emergency patch applied in response to an active exploit advisory

title: Mobile Device Patch Management
description: All mobile devices in scope, including corporate-owned and BYOD devices used to access corporate data, must be kept up to date with the latest operating system and application security patches within 14 days of release.
guidance: Mobile devices are increasingly targeted by sophisticated threats including zero-click exploits and malicious applications. Their patch management is often neglected compared to traditional endpoints because the update process is device-initiated. Cyber Essentials requires mobile devices accessing corporate resources to be subject to the same 14-day patching standard as other devices.
implementationGuide: Enrol all corporate mobile devices in Microsoft Intune or Jamf Pro for mobile (Jamf Now/School) and configure compliance policies that mark a device non-compliant if its OS version is more than one security patch level behind the current release. For BYOD, implement a Conditional Access policy that blocks access to corporate resources from devices that do not meet the patch compliance requirement. The 'done' state is a mobile compliance dashboard showing 100% of enrolled corporate devices and all BYOD devices accessing corporate data are on a supported, current OS patch level. A common failure mode is users delaying iOS or Android updates indefinitely because the device prompts them at inconvenient times — configure an Intune device compliance policy with a 14-day grace period before non-compliance is enforced and corporate access is blocked. Communicate the policy to users clearly and provide a self-service update guide.
exampleEvidence: Microsoft Intune or Jamf mobile compliance policy showing OS patch level requirement
Mobile device compliance dashboard report showing patch status of all enrolled devices
Conditional Access policy blocking non-compliant BYOD devices from corporate resources
List of devices outside the patch compliance window with enforcement action taken
User communication advising of mobile device patch compliance requirements
BYOD acceptable use policy requiring devices to be kept up to date as a condition of access
